questions.question_text: Which of the following license types does Louisiana issue for real estate professionals?
questions.correct_answer: Salesperson, broker, certified instructor
questions.explanation: Louisiana issues three types of real estate licenses: salesperson, broker, and certified instructor. Appraisers are licensed separately. Each license type has distinct education and experience requirements.

questions.question_text: An investor hires a friend to find and negotiate the purchase of rental properties, paying the friend a percentage of each deal. Does this activity require a license?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, brokering for pay requires a license
distractors: No, friends are exempt from licensing
Only if more than three properties are involved
Only if the friend is a licensed REALTOR
questions.explanation: Buying, selling, exchanging, or leasing real estate for others for compensation requires a Louisiana real estate license. The relationship between the parties does not create an exemption from licensing requirements.

questions.question_text: Which activity requires a Louisiana real estate license?
questions.correct_answer: Listing property for sale for compensation
questions.explanation: Listing, selling, buying, exchanging, or leasing real estate for others for compensation requires a license. Selling your own property, managing your own rentals, and appraising (separate license) do not require a real estate license.

questions.question_text: A property manager collects rent and negotiates leases for multiple property owners in exchange for a monthly fee. Is a real estate license required?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, leasing for others for compensation
distractors: Only if the properties are commercial
questions.explanation: Leasing real estate for others in exchange for compensation is a licensed activity in Louisiana. The number of units or property type does not create an exemption. A real estate license is required.

questions.question_text: A person exchanges properties on behalf of two clients and charges both a fee. Which aspect of this scenario triggers the licensing requirement?
questions.correct_answer: Exchanging property for others for pay
questions.explanation: The licensing trigger is performing real estate activities (listing, selling, buying, exchanging, leasing) for others for compensation. The exchange itself and dual representation are separate issues; it is the act of brokering for compensation that requires the license.

questions.question_text: What is the minimum age to obtain a Louisiana real estate salesperson license?
questions.correct_answer: 18 years old
questions.explanation: Applicants must be at least 18 years old to obtain a Louisiana salesperson license. Other requirements include a high school diploma or GED, 90 hours of pre-license education, and passing the licensing exam.

questions.question_text: How many hours of pre-license education must a Louisiana salesperson candidate complete?
questions.correct_answer: 90 hours
questions.explanation: Louisiana requires 90 hours of approved pre-license education for salesperson candidates. This education must be completed through an LREC-approved provider before the candidate is eligible to sit for the licensing exam.

questions.question_text: A salesperson applicant completed 90 hours of education and passed the exam. What additional step, required of all initial applicants since July 2023, must be completed before receiving a license?
questions.correct_answer: Fingerprint-based background check
questions.explanation: Under Act 553 of 2022, implemented by LREC effective July 3, 2023, all initial license and reinstatement applicants must complete a fingerprint-based criminal history review conducted by the Louisiana State Police and FBI. The applicant must also secure broker sponsorship and errors and omissions insurance before an active license is issued.

questions.question_text: A candidate passes the state exam but has not yet secured a sponsoring broker. Can LREC issue the salesperson license?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, but only as an inactive license
distractors: No, sponsorship is required before any license issues
Yes, as a fully active license
No, she must retake the exam after finding a sponsor
questions.explanation: Broker sponsorship (Affidavit of Sponsorship, Part B) is required only for an active license. If the Part B affidavit is not received within 90 days after the applicant passes the exam, LREC issues an inactive license (LAC 46:LXVII §303). An active license is not issued until the Transfer to Active Status Form is received and the errors and omissions insurance requirement is met.

questions.question_text: A 17-year-old with a GED completes 90 hours of pre-license education and wants to take the Louisiana salesperson exam. What prevents this applicant from obtaining a license?
questions.correct_answer: The applicant does not meet the age requirement
distractors: A GED is not accepted for licensure in Louisiana
90 hours is insufficient education for licensure
Minors cannot take the state licensing exam
questions.explanation: Louisiana requires applicants to be at least 18 years old. A GED is accepted as equivalent to a high school diploma, and 90 hours is the correct education requirement. The sole barrier here is age.

questions.question_text: How many years of active salesperson experience does Louisiana require before applying for a broker license?
questions.correct_answer: 4 years
questions.explanation: Louisiana requires 4 years of active salesperson experience to qualify for a broker license, with the last 2 years immediately preceding the application. The candidate must also complete 150 hours of education and pass the broker exam.

questions.question_text: A salesperson with 5 years of total experience was inactive for the most recent year. Does she qualify for a Louisiana broker license?
questions.correct_answer: No, the last 2 years must be active
distractors: Yes, only 3 total active years needed
No, all 4 years must be consecutive
questions.explanation: Louisiana requires that the last 2 years of the 4-year experience requirement be immediately preceding the broker application. Since the applicant was inactive for the most recent year, she does not meet this condition.

questions.question_text: How many hours of education must a broker candidate complete in Louisiana?
questions.correct_answer: 150 hours
questions.explanation: Louisiana requires 150 hours of approved education for broker candidates. This is 60 hours more than the 90-hour salesperson requirement. Candidates must also pass the broker exam and complete a background check.

questions.question_text: A salesperson has 4 years of active experience with the last 2 immediately before applying. She completed 120 hours of broker education. Can she sit for the broker exam?
questions.correct_answer: No, 150 hours of education are required
distractors: Yes, experience can substitute for hours
No, she needs 5 years of experience
questions.explanation: The broker education requirement is 150 hours. Although the applicant meets the experience requirement (4 years with the last 2 immediately preceding), she is 30 hours short on education and cannot sit for the exam.

questions.question_text: Which combination of requirements must a Louisiana broker candidate meet?
questions.correct_answer: 4 years experience, 150 hours, broker exam
questions.explanation: Louisiana broker candidates need 4 years of active salesperson experience (last 2 immediately before), 150 hours of approved education, a passing score on the broker exam, and a background check.

questions.question_text: After a candidate passes the Louisiana salesperson exam, within how many days must the broker's Affidavit of Sponsorship (Part B) reach LREC for an active license to be issued?
questions.correct_answer: 90 days
questions.explanation: The Affidavit of Sponsorship (Part B) may be submitted with the initial license application, but no later than 90 days after passing the license examination. If it is not received within the 90 days, LREC issues the applicant an inactive license rather than an active one (LAC 46:LXVII §303).

questions.question_text: A candidate fails the Louisiana state exam on her first attempt. How many retakes are permitted within one year?
questions.correct_answer: Unlimited retakes
questions.explanation: Louisiana permits unlimited exam attempts during the one-year examination authorization period, with the applicable exam fee paid for each attempt. Once the year runs out, the candidate must submit a new application and fees before testing again (LAC 46:LXVII §501, §507).

questions.question_text: A candidate receives an examination authorization from LREC but lets it expire without taking the exam. What must she do to become eligible to test?
questions.correct_answer: Submit a new application with the processing fee
distractors: Pay a $50 penalty to extend the old authorization
Complete the 90 hours of pre-license education again
Wait one year before she may reapply
questions.explanation: An LREC examination authorization is valid for one year. An applicant whose authorization expires before taking the exam receives a new authorization only upon submitting a new application and the processing fee prescribed in R.S. 37:1443 (LAC 46:LXVII §501). Education already completed does not have to be repeated.

questions.question_text: A candidate passes the national portion of the exam but fails the state portion. She does not pass the state portion within one year. What happens to her national score?
questions.correct_answer: The national score is invalidated
distractors: It remains valid for two additional years
It transfers to another state automatically
It is valid indefinitely once earned
questions.explanation: If a candidate passes one portion but does not pass the other within one year, the passing score is invalidated. The candidate must then retake both portions of the exam.

questions.question_text: A candidate passes the state portion on her third attempt within 8 months. She had previously passed the national portion on her first attempt. What is the status of her exam results?
questions.correct_answer: Both portions are valid for licensing
distractors: She must retake the national portion
Only the most recent score counts
questions.explanation: Both portions were passed within the one-year window, so both scores are valid. Louisiana allows unlimited retakes within one year, and passing one portion preserves that score as long as the other is passed within the same year.
